items.correct: Judging other cultures by the standards of one's own — assuming one's own cultural practices and values are normal or superior. It is a methodological problem in social research as well as a social phenomenon.
items.misconception: Ethnocentrism is the same as racism.
items.random_wrong: Ethnocentrism means concentrating on your own ethnic group only.
items.why: Ethnocentrism involves judgement using one's own framework, while racism asserts inherent inferiority. Researchers may inadvertently be ethnocentric without being racist. Students treat the two as synonyms, missing the methodological dimension.

items.correct: When studying hard-to-reach or hidden populations (drug users, undocumented migrants) where a sampling frame doesn't exist — participants recruit further participants. It sacrifices representativeness for access.
items.misconception: Snowball sampling is a type of random sampling.
items.random_wrong: Snowball sampling is only used in winter research.
items.why: Snowball sampling is the opposite of random — it relies on social networks, so participants share characteristics. Students confuse it with probability methods, missing why it is used (access) and its limit (representativeness).

items.correct: The unspoken norms, values, and behaviours taught implicitly in schools.
items.misconception: Secret lessons teachers deliberately hide from students.
items.why: Students take 'hidden' too literally as a conspiracy. It refers to things learned implicitly (punctuality, hierarchy, obedience) rather than explicitly taught content.

items.q: Top 1% own more than bottom what %?
items.why: It's 50%. “10%” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Inequality. “Income only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 90. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
